query: does UBE4B have any known role in promoting renal cell carcinoma

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The genetic silencing of the 3p25 genetic locus, which houses the Von Hippel landau tumor suppressor gene through various mechanisms that involve a point mutation, insertion, deletion, and epigenetic silencing (via promoter methylation), is the most frequently identified driving event in clear cell carcinoma. [33] [34] VHL is part of the E3 ligase complex. It is responsible for the proteasome-mediated degradation of hypoxia-inducible factors 1 alpha and 2 alpha by taking part in the ubiquitination of these molecules. [35] The accumulation of hypoxia-inducible proteins in an environment that is sufficiently oxygenated leads to an overexpression of genes involved in apoptosis, glycolysis, and angiogenesis. [36] Despite this underlying mechanism, which might lead one to attribute oncogenesis in clear cell carcinoma to a mutation in the VHL tumor suppressor gene only, it is essential to understand that this single event does not have the potential to lead to oncogenesis, as is evidenced by the long latent period, that precedes the development of tumors. [17] The primary pathway to be targeted in clear cell renal cancers includes the VEGF pathway due to VHL tumor suppressor gene loss located on the short arm of chromosome 3; however, other secondary targets may consist of mTOR c1, MET, and IL-8. [37] The presence of mutations in BAP 1 (histone deubiquitinase), PBRM1(involved in chromatin remodeling), SETD2 (histone methyltransferase), and KDM 5C and their association with prognosis has led to a proposal to classify renal cell carcinoma based on molecular pathogenesis. [38] Activating mutations in the mTOR-RHEB gene are also seen in clear cell tumors. [39] Both intratumor and intratumoral heterogeneity have been well documented. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common malignant tumor of the kidney and constitutes over 90 percent of all renal malignancies. RCC may be sporadic (~96%) or familial (4%) and is a heterogeneous group of disorders that are sub-classified into several distinct sub-types associated with distinct genetic abnormalities. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of renal cell carcinoma and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients of renal cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: VHL: VHL gene encodes a component of a ubiquitin ligase, which is involved in the degradation of hypoxia-induced factors (HIFs). These are transcription factors that alter the expression of genes in response to hypoxia. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder, is associated with loss-of-function germline mutations of this gene and poses a high risk of developing renal cell carcinoma and pheochromocytoma. [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Families at high risk for developing renal cancer were studied, which led to the cloning of genes. The genes whose alteration resulted in RCC formation were tumor suppressors (VHL, TSC) or oncogenes (MET).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In von-Hippel Lindau (VHL) disease, the loss of function mutation in the VHL tumor suppressor results in hemangioblastoma formation in multiple organs, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma—the VHL tumor suppressor gene codes for the VHL protein. The VHL protein is an E3 ubiquitin ligase that catalyzes the ubiquitination of hypoxia-inducible transcription factor-alpha (HIF1-alpha). HIF-alpha regulates erythropoietin (EPO) production and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). Under normal physiologic conditions, HIF1-alpha remains hydroxylated. In the hydroxylated form, it can be recognized and degraded by the VHL protein. This results in the prevention of EPO and VEGF induction under normoxic conditions. In VHL disease, the gene mutation results in the VHL protein’s inability to bind HIF-alpha, leading to uncontrolled growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Current studies implicate the VHL gene in the development of both sporadic and familial type of clear cell-RCC (CCRCC). [4] [5] Mutation in the MET gene is a feature of the familial type of papillary-RCC (PRCC). [4] Although sporadic PRCC has a much wider set of molecular alterations associated with it. [4] [6] [7] About 4% of renal cell cancers are the result of rare hereditary conditions as detailed below:

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A defective VHL gene results in the interaction among intracellular proteins resulting in the upregulation of messenger RNA (mRNA) encoding for growth factors such as platelet-derived growth factor-beta (PDGFB), vascular endothelial cell growth factor (VEGF), and transforming growth factor-alpha (TGFA). All these growth factors promote the development of these vascular tumors. Under normal oxygen conditions, HIF1A and HIF2A undergo hydroxylation and binds to a protein called pVHL and are degraded. In hypoxemic conditions or the absence of pVHL, HIFA accumulates, leading to the upregulation of mRNA and increased production of the growth factors. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cell loses protection against genetic alterations when P53 mutations occur. Ubiquitin ligase HDM2 and P53 interact with E3 and E4 proteins, which induce polyubiquitination and degradation of P53, which prevents apoptosis. [14] [15] They are also associated with the overexpression of UBE4B and with the amplification of these genes in brain tumors. The absence of P53 mutations in some families indicates that P53 protein may undergo posttranslational modifications, or other genes may be involved. Genetic copy number variation, used as an indicator of genetic instability, are higher in patients with germline TP53 mutations than in healthy individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the histopathological findings of clear cell renal cancer. Assess the role of history taking, physical examination, and radiological investigations in evaluating and managing clear cell renal cancer. Evaluate the options available in the management of clear cell renal cancer. Communicate interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and advancing patient-related outcomes in clear cell renal carcinoma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Mass -- Evaluation -- Renal Mass Genetic Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: A genetic analysis will be needed in some patients with renal cell malignancies. This is recommended in the following situations: [35] Bilateral or multiple tumors Individuals with related disorders who are known to be predisposed to renal cell cancer Patients with a positive family history of renal cell carcinoma (first or second-degree relatives) Therapeutic failures Younger patients

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Clear cell renal cancer is the most common type of kidney cancer, comprising 80 percent of all malignant tumors found within the kidney. It is known as the internist tumor and is well known to cause a wide spectrum of paraneoplastic manifestations to mimic other tumors and benign conditions. This activity describes the evaluation and management of this aggressive tumor while also trying to highlight the role of the interprofessional team in the comprehensive management of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: While most of the cases of clear cell carcinoma are sporadic, 2 to 3% of the cases are related to genetic alterations in the von Hippel landau gene, chromatin remodeling genes/epigenetic regulators (Polybromo 1, BRCA protein 1, and SET D1), and genetic alterations in pathways that disrupt signal transduction involving the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway. [16] [17] PTEN deficiency is usually associated with a more aggressive phenotype. [18] While angiomyolipoma (AML) is the most common renal neoplasm seen in patients with tuberous sclerosis, clear cell tumors may be seen rarely. [19] Clear cell tumors can also be seen in a small minority of patients with Birt Hogg Dube syndrome, who have been otherwise shown to develop hybrid-chromophobe tumors and oncocytomas. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Oncocytoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the typical presentation of renal oncocytoma. Explain how to rule out conditions that may mimic renal oncocytoma. Review the management of renal oncocytoma. Explain the need for optimizing care coordination, with particular emphasis on communication between interprofessional medical teams, to enhance prompt evaluation and management of renal oncocytomas to decrease risk of metastases and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Renal cell carcinoma can develop due to genetic or acquired causes. The two most common genetic abnormalities are the Von Hippel–Lindau (VHL) gene and the protein polybromo-1 (PBRM-1) gene. The common acquired causes include long-term dialysis dependence, history of smoking, long-term analgesic use, obesity, hypertension, and diabetes. [2] [3] About 95% of the patients with ccRCC have a deletion in the short arm of chromosome 3 (loss of 3p). Screening for this is usually not recommended in healthy individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The genetic basis of PRCC is mainly based on the inherited form of the disease. The cytogenetic abnormalities associated with the PRCC subtype include trisomy of chromosomes 3, 7, 12, 16, 17, and 20, c-MET mutations and loss of the Y chromosome. PRCC has two main subtypes (see histopathology section). About 15% of nonhereditary PRCC and hereditary PRCC (usually type 1) are characterized by germline MET alteration or gain of chromosome 7. Type 2 PRCC is further grouped based on multiple and distinct genetic alterations in the form of activation of the NRF2-ARE pathway, CDKN2A loss, SETD2 mutations, and TFE3 fusion. A CIMP (CpG island methylator phenotype) suggests a poor prognosis. Hereditary leiomyomatosis associated PRCC have FH mutation and are aggressive tumors. [4] [6] [7] [17] Chromophobe RCC (ChRCC) has a number of cytogenetic abnormalities associated with it that include loss of multiple chromosomes such as 1, 2, 6, 10, 13, 17, and 21. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in the VHL gene can be transmitted in an autosomal dominant fashion or in a sporadic way. More than 50% of the patients with ccRCC sporadically have VHL gene abnormalities. [6] ouble hit theory is that some patients are born with one copy of defective VHL gene, and the second hit is due to the somatic mutation. Normally, VHL is a tumor suppressor gene that regulates the levels of intracellular proteins and hypoxia-inducible factor 1 alpha and 2 alpha (HIF1A and HIF2A). [7] [8]

Nephrectomy is commonly performed for renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية). Recognizing that an unspecified malignant neoplasm can necessitate nephrectomy and that this procedure is frequently associated with these specific renal cancers supports integrated diagnostic and therapeutic planning. Collectively, these relationships delineate how metastatic lesions, secondary lesions, and malignant cellular infiltrates interrelate across diverse organ systems, how benign proliferations are distinguished from malignant disease, and how multilingual terminologies and hierarchical classifications aid clinicians in recognizing patterns of cancer spread, interpreting pathology, and organizing diagnoses within a comprehensive cancer ontology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2880
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells constitute the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis, indicating that these neoplasms arise from the proliferation of transformed, malignant cells within the renal parenchyma and renal pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2714
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant are the abnormal cells that define cancer of kidney and renal pelvis, and this specific malignancy is mapped to the Arabic term سرطان الخلايا الكلوية. Recognizing that malignant tumor cells constitute the pathological cellular basis of this renal and pelvic cancer and linking the English disease name to its Arabic equivalent supports accurate diagnosis, interdisciplinary communication, and culturally appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrectomy; partial or complete, known in Arabic as استئصال الكلية, is a surgical procedure that involves the removal of part or all of a kidney. This type of kidney removal is frequently performed in the context of renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية), reflecting its central role in the treatment of this kidney cancer. The close association between استئصال الكلية and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية highlights the importance of nephrectomy in managing patients with renal cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1644
retrieved_docs.content: Fanconi syndrome, other diseases of the kidney and ureters, and end‑stage renal disease are clinically associated with one another. Fanconi syndrome is linked to end‑stage renal disease, indicating that patients with this tubular disorder may progress to or coexist with advanced kidney failure. In addition, Fanconi syndrome is clinically associated with other kidney and ureter diseases, suggesting frequent co‑occurrence with a broader spectrum of renal pathology. Likewise, other diseases of the kidney and ureters are associated with end‑stage renal disease, reflecting their potential to culminate in severe loss of renal function. Together, these interconnections highlight a network of renal conditions that often coexist and may collectively contribute to the development of end‑stage renal disease, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of kidney‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are not considered a disease‑finding for cancer of kidney and renal pelvis, indicating that the presence of benign tumor cells does not constitute evidence of this malignancy. Cancer of kidney and renal pelvis is also identified by its Arabic term الورم الأورمي الكلوي.

Collectively, these interrelated concepts map oncologic terminology, hierarchical disease relationships, cellular pathology, metastatic pathways, linguistic variations, and associated clinical interventions, providing clinicians with a unified framework for recognizing patterns of cancer spread, interpreting pathological findings, and applying consistent terminology across diverse organ‑specific and unspecified malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 835
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrectomy; partial or complete, referred to in Arabic as استئصال الكلية, is a surgical procedure that involves the removal of part or all of a kidney. This operation is frequently performed in the setting of renal neoplasms, specifically الورم الأورمي الكلوي (renal oncocytoma) and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية (renal cell carcinoma). The frequent co‑occurrence of استئصال الكلية with both الورم الأورمي الكلوي and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية highlights the clinical practice of managing these kidney tumors through partial or total kidney resection, informing treatment planning and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: illustrates that the thymus, an endocrine organ, can serve as a metastatic destination. Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.
